questions.text: Which organization is a multinational body dedicated to worldwide agreement on international standards and developed the OSI model?
questions.explanation: Established in 1947, the International Standards Organization (ISO) is the organization that developed the Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model.

questions.text: How many layers are defined in the Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model?
questions.explanation: The OSI model consists of seven layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, and Application.

questions.text: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for the movement of individual bits from one hop to the next?
questions.explanation: The physical layer is responsible for the movement of individual bits from one hop (node) to the next.

questions.text: The process of dividing the stream of bits received from the network layer into manageable units is called:
questions.explanation: In the data link layer, framing is the process of dividing the bit stream into manageable data units called frames.

questions.text: Which layer is responsible for the delivery of individual packets from the source host to the destination host?
questions.explanation: The network layer is responsible for the source-to-destination delivery of a packet, possibly across multiple networks.

questions.text: The transport layer is responsible for the delivery of a message from:
questions.explanation: The transport layer is responsible for process-to-process delivery of the entire message.

questions.text: Which of the following functions is performed by the session layer?
questions.explanation: The session layer is responsible for dialog control (half-duplex/full-duplex) and synchronization (adding checkpoints).

questions.text: Encryption and compression are primary functions of the:
questions.explanation: The presentation layer is responsible for translation, encryption, and compression of data.

questions.text: The application layer provides which of the following services?
questions.explanation: The application layer provides services like remote log-on (NVT), file management (FTAM), email (Mail Services), and directory services.

questions.text: Which protocol in the TCP/IP suite is used to find the physical address of a node when its internet address (IP) is known?
questions.explanation: The Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) is used to associate a logical IP address with a physical MAC address.

questions.text: In the TCP/IP protocol suite, how many levels of addresses are used?
questions.explanation: TCP/IP employs four levels of addresses: physical, logical, port, and specific.

questions.text: A physical address in most local-area networks is ________ bits long.
questions.explanation: Most LANs use a 48-bit (6-byte) physical address, usually represented as 12 hexadecimal digits.

questions.text: A port address in the TCP/IP protocol suite is ________ bits long.
questions.explanation: As mentioned in the text, a port address is a 16-bit address represented by one decimal number.

questions.text: As data travels from source to destination, which addresses change from hop to hop?
questions.explanation: The physical addresses change from hop to hop, while logical and port addresses usually remain the same.

questions.text: Which protocol provides support for newer applications such as VOIP and combines the best features of UDP and TCP?
questions.explanation: Stream Control Transmission Protocol (SCTP) provides support for VOIP and combines features of both UDP and TCP.

questions.text: The Internetworking Protocol (IP) is characterized as a(n) ________ protocol.
questions.explanation: IP is an unreliable and connectionless protocol that provides 'best-effort' delivery.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a specific address?
questions.explanation: Specific addresses are user-friendly addresses like email addresses and URLs.

questions.text: In the data link layer, detecting and retransmitting damaged or lost frames is part of:
questions.explanation: Error control in the data link layer involves detecting and retransmitting lost or damaged frames across a single link.

questions.text: Which layer is responsible for adding checkpoints (synchronization points) to a stream of data?
questions.explanation: The session layer allows a process to add checkpoints to a stream of data for synchronization.

questions.text: When comparing TCP/IP to the OSI model, which layers of OSI are usually combined into a single Application layer in TCP/IP?
questions.explanation: In the five-layer TCP/IP model, the top layer (Application) performs the tasks of the Session, Presentation, and Application layers of the OSI model.

questions.text: Which protocol is used by a host to discover its Internet address when it knows only its physical address?
questions.explanation: The Reverse Address Resolution Protocol (RARP) allows a host to discover its Internet address given its physical address.

questions.text: Which layer defines which device has control over the link at any given time?
questions.explanation: Access control is a function of the data link layer that determines which device has control over the link.

questions.text: What is the primary function of the ICMP protocol?

choices.label: C
choices.text: To send query and error reporting messages

questions.explanation: The Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP) is used to send query and error reporting messages.

questions.text: Which address is used to identify a specific process running on a computer?
questions.explanation: The port address (or service-point address) identifies a specific process (running program) on a computer.

questions.text: What does the term 'Hop-to-Hop' delivery usually refer to?
questions.explanation: Hop-to-hop delivery refers to moving data units from one node to the next across a single link, which is the responsibility of the data link layer.

questions.text: In the TCP/IP suite, which transport layer protocol is connection-oriented and reliable?
questions.explanation: Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) is a reliable, connection-oriented protocol.

questions.text: What is the function of the IGMP protocol?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Simultaneous transmission to a group of recipients

questions.explanation: The Internet Group Message Protocol (IGMP) facilitates the simultaneous transmission of a message to a group of recipients.

questions.text: The 12-digit hexadecimal address `07:01:02:01:2C:4B` is an example of a:
questions.explanation: A physical address (MAC address) is typically 48 bits (6 bytes) written as 12 hexadecimal digits.

questions.text: Routing or switching packets to their final destination is a function of which layer?
questions.explanation: The network layer is responsible for routing packets from the source to the final destination.

questions.text: Which layer is responsible for the division of a message into segments and their subsequent reassembly?
questions.explanation: The transport layer performs segmentation and reassembly of messages.

questions.text: Translation involves changing information from a sender-dependent format into a ________ format.
questions.explanation: As part of the presentation layer's translation function, information is converted from a sender-dependent format to a common format.

questions.text: Which of the following describes 'Encapsulation' in the OSI model?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The process of moving bits across a physical wire.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The process of adding headers to data at each layer.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The process of reassembling segments into a message.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The process of routing packets based on IP addresses.

questions.explanation: Encapsulation occurs as data moves from higher layers to lower layers, with each layer adding its own header (and sometimes trailer).

questions.text: Which layer handles 'Flow Control' between a single link's sender and receiver?
questions.explanation: Flow control across a single link is the responsibility of the data link layer. (Transport layer handles end-to-end flow control).

questions.text: The original TCP/IP protocol suite was defined as having ________ layers.
questions.explanation: The original TCP/IP protocol suite was defined with four layers: host-to-network, internet, transport, and application.

questions.text: In the five-layer TCP/IP model, the 'Internet' layer of the original suite is referred to as the ________ layer.
questions.explanation: In modern terminology, the Internet layer of the TCP/IP suite is called the Network layer.

questions.text: Which layer is responsible for reducing the number of bits in information, which is particularly important for multimedia?
questions.explanation: Data compression, which reduces the number of bits, is a function of the presentation layer.

questions.text: What happens to the port address as a packet travels from source to destination?

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is removed after the first link.

questions.explanation: While physical addresses change hop-to-hop, logical and port addresses generally remain the same from source to destination.

questions.text: Which layer allows a user to access files in a remote host?
questions.explanation: File Transfer, Access, and Management (FTAM) is an application layer service.

questions.text: Peer-to-peer processes in the OSI model describe communication between:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adjacent layers on the same machine

choices.label: C
choices.text: A client and a server only

questions.explanation: Peer-to-peer processes refer to communication between the same layer on different devices through established protocols.

questions.text: Which address type is typically written as a URL like `www.mhhe.com`?
questions.explanation: URLs and email addresses are examples of specific addresses.

questions.text: Which of the following best defines a transmission medium in data communications?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A device that converts digital signals to analog signals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anything that can carry information from a source to a destination.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The protocol used to govern the flow of data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A software layer that manages network traffic.

questions.explanation: As stated in the material, a transmission medium is anything capable of carrying information from a source to a destination.

questions.text:
How are transmission media broadly classified in networking?

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Transmission Media] --> B[Guided Media]
    A --> C[Unguided Media]
```
questions.explanation: The text classifies media into Guided media (cables) and Unguided media (wireless).

questions.text: In a twisted-pair cable, what is the primary purpose of twisting the two copper conductors together?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To make the cable more flexible and easier to install.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To increase the physical strength of the wire.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To reduce the effect of noise and crosstalk.

questions.explanation: Twisting reduces the effect of external interference (noise) and crosstalk between adjacent pairs.

questions.text: Which type of twisted-pair cable contains a metal foil or braided-mesh covering to further reduce interference?
questions.explanation: Shielded Twisted-Pair (STP) has a metal covering to block electromagnetic interference.

questions.text: What is the function of the 'local loop' in telephone networks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It connects the backbone of the internet to the ISP.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It connects individual subscribers to the central telephone office.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It handles the routing of calls between different cities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It modulates the signal for fiber-optic transmission.

questions.explanation: The local loop is the twisted-pair line that connects the subscriber's premises to the central office.

questions.text: Coaxial cable carries signals of higher frequency ranges than twisted-pair cable. What is the role of its outer conductor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It only acts as a physical protector for the inner core.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It serves as both a shield against noise and a second conductor to complete the circuit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It transmits the light pulses for high-speed data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is used only for grounding purposes and does not carry signal.

questions.explanation: In coaxial cables, the outer conductor acts as a shield and also serves as the second conductor in the circuit.

questions.text: Which connector is specifically used to prevent the reflection of a signal at the end of a coaxial cable in an Ethernet network?
questions.explanation: The BNC terminator is placed at the ends of a coaxial cable bus to absorb the signal and prevent reflection.

questions.text: A fiber-optic cable consists of a core surrounded by a cladding. Which statement is true regarding their densities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The core is less dense than the cladding.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The core is more dense than the cladding.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The core and cladding have identical densities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Density does not matter; only the material color matters.

questions.explanation: For total internal reflection to occur in fiber optics, the core must be more dense (higher refractive index) than the cladding.

questions.text: In fiber-optic propagation modes, what characterizes 'Graded-index' multimode fiber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The density of the core remains constant from center to edge.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Light travels in a straight line through a very small core.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The density is highest at the center and decreases gradually toward the edge.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The fiber uses a metal shield to prevent light leakage.

questions.explanation: Graded-index fiber has a core with varying density, being highest at the center.

questions.text: Why is 'Single mode' fiber preferred for long-distance communication compared to multimode fiber?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It uses multiple beams of light to increase speed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has a larger core diameter, making it easier to connect.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The propagation of beams is almost horizontal and delays are negligible due to its small diameter.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is made of cheaper plastic materials.

questions.explanation: Single mode fiber has a very small core, allowing only one mode of light to propagate, which minimizes delays and signal distortion.

questions.text: Which of the following is a significant disadvantage of fiber-optic cable?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Complexity of installation and high cost.

questions.explanation: As noted in the disadvantages, cost, installation, and maintenance are the primary drawbacks of fiber optics.

questions.text: Which unguided media propagation method involves signals reflecting off the ionosphere to reach greater distances?
questions.explanation: Sky propagation involves higher frequency radio waves radiating upward and reflecting off the ionosphere.

questions.text: What is a characteristic of radio waves in the frequency range of 3 kHz to 1 GHz?

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are omnidirectional and can penetrate walls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They require line-of-sight antennas facing each other.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are exclusively used for short-range indoor communication.

questions.explanation: Radio waves are omnidirectional (propagate in all directions) and have the ability to penetrate walls.

questions.text: Microwaves (1 GHz to 300 GHz) differ from radio waves because they are:
questions.explanation: Microwaves are unidirectional, meaning sending and receiving antennas must be aligned.

questions.text: Which frequency band is typically used for short-range communication in a closed area, such as a TV remote control?
questions.explanation: Infrared signals are used for short-range communication in closed areas (300 GHz to 400 THz).

questions.text: Why can infrared signals not be used for outdoor communication?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The sun's rays contain infrared waves that cause interference.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They penetrate walls and cause privacy issues.

questions.explanation: The text states that the sun's rays interfere with infrared communication, making it unsuitable for outdoor use.

questions.text: Which category of UTP cable is considered the lowest quality?
questions.explanation: UTP cable quality is determined by category numbers, with 1 being the lowest and 7 the highest.

questions.text: What does the 'RG' rating in coaxial cables stand for?
questions.explanation: Coaxial cables are categorized by Radio Government (RG) ratings.

questions.text: Which connector is the same size as an RJ45 but used for fiber-optic cables?
questions.explanation: The MT-RJ is a fiber-optic connector that is approximately the same size as the RJ45 connector used for UTP.

questions.text: What is the primary advantage of 'Line-of-sight' propagation used for very high-frequency signals?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Signals hug the curvature of the earth.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Signals are transmitted in straight lines directly from antenna to antenna.

questions.explanation: Line-of-sight propagation transmits signals in straight lines between directional antennas.

questions.text: Which material is mentioned as being used in fiber-optic cable construction for its strength, also used in bulletproof vests?
questions.explanation: Kevlar is used in fiber-optic cable construction to provide physical strength.

questions.text: In multimode fiber optics, what is 'Step-index'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The core density decreases gradually toward the cladding.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The core density remains constant from the center to the edges.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The light travels only in the center of the core.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The core is made of a series of physical steps.

questions.explanation: Step-index means the refractive index (density) is uniform throughout the core and changes abruptly at the cladding interface.

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding attenuation in transmission media?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fiber-optic cable has more attenuation than coaxial cable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coaxial cable has much higher attenuation than twisted-pair cable.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Twisted-pair cable has the lowest attenuation among all guided media.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fiber-optic cable has 10 times more repeaters than copper.

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that attenuation is much higher in coaxial cables than in twisted-pair cables, and fiber-optic has the least.

questions.text: The SC (Subscriber Channel) connector is commonly used for which application?
questions.explanation: The Subscriber Channel (SC) connector is used for cable TV applications.

questions.text: What is the frequency range for Microwaves?
questions.explanation: Microwaves range from 1 GHz to 300 GHz.

questions.text: Which type of antenna is used for radio waves to propagate signals in all directions?
questions.explanation: Radio waves use omnidirectional antennas to broadcast in all directions.

questions.text: Ground propagation is characteristic of signals with which frequency?
questions.explanation: Ground propagation involves low-frequency signals that hug the earth's surface.

questions.text: Which guided medium is immune to electromagnetic interference (EMI)?
questions.explanation: Fiber-optic cables use light instead of electricity, making them immune to EMI.

questions.text: A BNC T connector is used primarily to:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Connect a television to an antenna.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Branch out a connection to a computer in an Ethernet network.

questions.explanation: BNC T connectors allow a coaxial cable network to branch out to connect to devices like computers.

questions.text: What does the term 'multicast' refer to in the context of radio waves?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sending a message to one specific recipient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sending signals to multiple recipients (like radio/TV broadcasting).

questions.explanation: Multicast communications, like radio and TV, involve one sender and multiple receivers.

questions.text: In a fiber-optic cable, what is the 'cladding'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The outer plastic jacket protecting the cable from weather.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A layer of less dense glass or plastic surrounding the core.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The copper wire at the center of the cable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The electrical shield that prevents EMI.

questions.explanation: Cladding is the layer of lower refractive index material that surrounds the core to ensure light remains trapped via reflection.

questions.text: Which application is most likely to use 'Unicast' microwave communication?
questions.explanation: Microwaves are used for unicast communication such as cellular telephones and satellite networks.

questions.text: Which of the following describes the relationship between signal power and distance in ground propagation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The greater the power, the greater the distance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Higher power causes the signal to fly into space.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Power only affects the frequency, not the distance.

questions.explanation: In ground propagation, more power allows the signal to travel further along the earth's surface.

questions.text: What is the primary advantage of Radio waves being able to penetrate walls?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It allows receivers to get signals inside buildings.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It increases the data rate of the signal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It eliminates the need for an antenna.

questions.explanation: The ability to penetrate walls is an advantage because it enables indoor reception of external signals.

questions.text: A 'unidirectional' antenna is typically used for which type of communication?
questions.explanation: Microwaves are unidirectional and require focused, aligned antennas.

questions.text: In a twisted-pair cable, why is one wire used for grounding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To prevent electric shocks to the user.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To act as a reference for the receiver to detect the signal difference.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To increase the weight of the cable.

questions.explanation: One wire carries the signal, while the other is used as a ground reference; the receiver detects the difference between the two.

questions.text: What does 'Gauge' measure in the context of UTP cables?
questions.explanation: Gauge is a standard measure of the thickness of a wire.

questions.text: Compared to twisted-pair, coaxial cable provides:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Higher bandwidth and higher frequency ranges.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immunity to all forms of attenuation.

questions.explanation: Coaxial cable is designed to carry higher frequency signals and offers much higher bandwidth than twisted-pair.

questions.text: Which connector is typically used for connecting fiber-optic cable to networking devices?
questions.explanation: The Straight-tip (ST) connector is used for connecting fiber cables to networking devices.

questions.text: The frequency range of Infrared is:
questions.explanation: Infrared signals range from 300 GHz to 400 THz.

questions.text: What is the primary function of a switch in a switched network?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To provide permanent physical connections between all devices.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To create temporary connections between two or more devices linked to it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To act as an end system like a telephone or computer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To increase the bandwidth of the physical medium.

questions.explanation: Switches are devices capable of creating temporary connections between two or more devices linked to the switch.

questions.text: In a switched network, some nodes are connected to end systems while others are used specifically for:
questions.explanation: According to the text, some nodes are connected to end systems, while others are used only for routing.

questions.text:
Based on the taxonomy of switched networks provided in the text, how are packet-switched networks further classified?

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Switched Networks] --> B[Circuit-Switched Networks]
    A --> C[Packet-Switched Networks]
    C --> D[Datagram Networks]
    C --> E[Virtual-Circuit Networks]
```
questions.explanation: Packet-switched networks are divided into Datagram and Virtual-Circuit networks.

questions.text: At which layer of the OSI model does circuit switching primarily take place?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'Circuit switching takes place at the physical layer.'

questions.text: What is a defining characteristic of a connection in a circuit-switched network?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It uses all available channels on a link for one user.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a dedicated path using one dedicated channel on each link.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It shares resources on-demand without reservation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It divides data into small packets for transmission.

questions.explanation: A connection is a dedicated path made of one or more links, and each connection uses only one dedicated channel on each link.

questions.text: Which methods are typically used to divide a physical link into \( n \) channels in circuit switching?
questions.explanation: Each link is normally divided into n channels by using Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) or Time Division Multiplexing (TDM).

questions.text: In a circuit-switched network, when is addressing used?
questions.explanation: The text states: 'Addressing is used only during the setup phase' for circuit switching.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the three phases of circuit switching?
questions.explanation: The three phases are Setup, Data Transfer, and Teardown.

questions.text: Why is circuit switching considered to have low efficiency?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because resources remain allocated even if no data is being sent.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because the setup phase takes too much bandwidth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because it uses complex routing tables.

questions.explanation: Resources are allocated for the entire duration; if there is no activity, the resources are unavailable to others, leading to low efficiency.

questions.text: Regarding delay in circuit-switched networks, which statement is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There is a significant waiting time at each intermediate node.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delay is high due to packetization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The delay is minimal because there is no waiting time at nodes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Delay is only caused by the teardown phase.

questions.explanation: In circuit switching, the delay is minimal as there is no waiting time at each node once the connection is established.

questions.text: Consider a setup phase in a circuit-switched network with Systems A and M. What is required for the connection to be officially established?

choices.label: A
choices.text: System A sends the setup request.

choices.label: C
choices.text: System A receives an ACK from System M.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The data transfer phase begins immediately after the request.

questions.explanation: The connection is established only after System A receives the ACK (Acknowledgment) from System M.

questions.text: If a circuit-switched link uses FDM to connect a maximum of two 4-kHz voice channels, what is the total bandwidth of that link?
questions.explanation: If each channel is 4 kHz and there are two channels, the bandwidth is \( 2 \times 4 = 8 \) kHz.

questions.text: In the T-1 line example with two 4x8 switches, how many output ports are used to allow communication between the two different offices?
questions.explanation: The example states: 'Four other output ports allow communication between the two offices.'

questions.text: Datagram switching is implemented at which layer of the OSI model?
questions.explanation: Datagram switching is done at the network layer.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a datagram network?

choices.label: D
choices.text: All packets must follow the same path.

questions.explanation: Datagram networks are connectionless; they have no setup or teardown phases, and each packet is treated independently.

questions.text: In a datagram network, what is the basis for the routing table in a switch?
questions.explanation: A switch in a datagram network uses a routing table that is based on the destination address.

questions.text: What happens to the destination address in the header of a packet as it travels through a datagram network?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is stripped off after the first hop.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It remains the same during the entire journey.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is replaced by a local VCI.

questions.explanation: The destination address in the header of a packet in a datagram network remains the same during the entire journey of the packet.

questions.text: How are resources allocated in a datagram network?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Equally divided among all active users.

questions.explanation: In a datagram network, resources are allocated on demand, on a first-come, first-served basis.

questions.text: Which component contributes to the 'Greater delay' in datagram networks compared to circuit switching?
questions.explanation: Datagram networks have greater delay due to 'Waiting Time at Switches' (queuing), in addition to transmission and propagation time.

questions.text: Why is datagram switching considered highly efficient for computer communications?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because resources are only allocated when packets are actually transferred.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because it uses a fixed path for all messages.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because it eliminates the need for routing tables.

questions.explanation: Resources are allocated only when needed. If a source is idle, the resources can be reallocated to other packets, providing high efficiency.

questions.text: A virtual-circuit network is described as a cross between:
questions.explanation: The text states: 'A virtual-circuit network is a cross between a circuit-switched network and a datagram network.'

questions.text: In which layer is virtual-circuit switching typically implemented in a switched WAN?
questions.explanation: Virtual-circuit techniques are normally implemented in the data link layer.

questions.text: What is a VCI in the context of virtual-circuit networks?
questions.explanation: VCI stands for Virtual-Circuit Identifier.

questions.text: The scope of a Virtual-Circuit Identifier (VCI) is:
questions.explanation: The VCI is a small number that has only 'switch scope'. When a frame leaves a switch, it often gets a different VCI.

questions.text: When is a 'Global Address' used in a virtual-circuit network?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In every packet header during data transfer.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only to create a virtual circuit identifier during setup.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To identify switches during the teardown phase.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is never used; only VCIs are used.

questions.explanation: Global addressing is used only to create a virtual circuit identifier (during setup).

questions.text: Which phase involves all switches deleting the corresponding entry from their tables in a VC network?
questions.explanation: In the teardown phase, switches delete the corresponding entry from their table after receiving the teardown request/confirmation.

questions.text: In virtual-circuit switching, although all packets follow the same path, why might they arrive with different delays?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because they are treated independently like datagrams.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If resource allocation is on demand.

questions.explanation: Packets follow the same path but may have different delays if resource allocation is on demand rather than reserved.

questions.text: Which switching method uses a 'Routing Table' based on 'Destination Address'?
questions.explanation: Datagram switches use routing tables based on destination addresses.

questions.text:
Match the technology to its primary OSI layer:
1. Circuit Switching
2. Datagram Switching
3. Virtual-Circuit Switching
questions.explanation: Circuit switching = Physical layer; Datagram = Network layer; Virtual-circuit = Data Link layer.

questions.text: What is the specific name of the acknowledgment frame sent by the receiver in the setup phase of a Virtual-Circuit network?
questions.explanation: The text identifies it as the 'Setup Acknowledgement (Receiver)'.

questions.text: In a datagram network, what does 'connectionless' imply?

choices.label: B
choices.text: No setup or teardown phases are required.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sender and receiver are not connected to the same switch.

questions.explanation: Connectionless networks mean there are no setup or teardown phases.

questions.text: Efficiency is highest in which of the following switching methods?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Efficiency depends only on the switch hardware

questions.explanation: Datagram switching has 'High efficiency' because resources are allocated only when needed, whereas circuit switching has 'Low efficiency'.

questions.text: In virtual-circuit switching, when a frame arrives at a switch, it carries a VCI. What happens to this VCI when the frame leaves the switch?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is removed and replaced by a global address.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It remains the same to ensure end-to-end delivery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is changed to a different VCI based on the switch table.

questions.explanation: When a frame arrives it has a VCI; when it leaves, it has a different VCI.

questions.text: Which type of switching is used in traditional telephone networks?
questions.explanation: Switching at the physical layer in the traditional telephone network uses the circuit-switching approach.

questions.text: In circuit switching, if a computer is connected to another but there is no activity for a long time, what happens to the reserved resources?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are automatically released for others.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They remain unavailable to other connections.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are shared with other computers on-demand.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The switch turns off the link.

questions.explanation: Resources are allocated during the entire duration of the connection and are unavailable to others even if there is no activity.

questions.text: Which phase in virtual-circuit switching involves the sender sending a request that includes the global source and destination addresses?
questions.explanation: The setup phase uses the setup request (sender) which includes global addresses.

questions.text: Total delay in a datagram network includes which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transmission Time + Propagation Time + Waiting Time

choices.label: C
choices.text: Setup Time + Data Transfer Time + Teardown Time

questions.explanation: Figure 8.9 and the text list Transmission, Propagation, and Waiting Time at switches as components of delay in a datagram network.

questions.text: Why do packets in a datagram network need to be divided into fixed or variable sizes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To increase the speed of propagation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To follow the requirements of the governing protocol.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To ensure they all follow the same path.

questions.explanation: The size of the packet is determined by the network and the governing protocol.

questions.text:
Which diagram represent the data flow in a Virtual-Circuit setup phase?

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Source
    participant SW as Switch
    participant D as Destination
    S->>SW: Setup Request (Global Addr)
    SW->>D: Setup Request
    D-->>SW: Setup Acknowledgement (VCI)
    SW-->>S: Setup Acknowledgement (VCI)
```

choices.label: B
choices.text: It represents the setup phase where VCIs are established.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It represents the routing process in a datagram network.

questions.explanation: The diagram illustrates the setup phase where a request is sent and an ACK (containing the VCI) is returned.

questions.text: In a datagram network, what happens if a resource is idle for several minutes between packets?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The resource can be reallocated for other packets from other sources.

questions.explanation: In datagram networks, resources can be reallocated during periods of inactivity between packets.

questions.text: Which of these is a characteristic of Virtual-Circuit networks that is identical to Circuit-Switched networks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Resources are always reserved for maximum efficiency.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There are setup and teardown phases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Addressing is done in the physical layer.

questions.explanation: Both circuit-switched and virtual-circuit networks have setup and teardown phases.

questions.text: What is the primary drawback of datagram networks regarding delay?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Zero delay makes it hard to synchronize.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Greater delay due to waiting times at switches.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Propagation delay is twice as much as circuit switching.

questions.explanation: Wait times at switches (queuing) cause greater delay in datagram networks compared to circuit switching.

questions.text: The term 'Switched WAN' is most commonly associated with which technique in the data link layer?
questions.explanation: Switching at the data link layer in a switched WAN is normally implemented using virtual-circuit techniques.

questions.text: What determines the size of a packet in a packet-switched network?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The distance between source and destination.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of switches in the path.

questions.explanation: Packet size is determined by the network and the governing protocol.

questions.text: In virtual-circuit switching, the 'Global address' is used specifically to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Verify the integrity of the data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Create the entry in the switch table for the VCI.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Route packets if the VCI fails.

questions.explanation: Global addresses are used during the setup phase to establish the virtual circuit and fill the switch tables.

questions.text: Which term describes communication that occurs at a distance?
questions.explanation: The term 'telecommunication' specifically means communication at a distance.

questions.text: The effectiveness of a data communications system depends on four fundamental characteristics: Delivery, Accuracy, Timeliness, and **____**.
questions.explanation: Jitter refers to the variation in packet arrival time, which is a key characteristic for effective data communication, especially in real-time applications.

questions.text: In a data communications system, what is the 'physical path' by which a message travels called?
questions.explanation: The transmission medium is the physical path (like wire, cable, or fiber) that carries the message from sender to receiver.

questions.text: Which data flow mode is characterized by unidirectional communication, like a keyboard to a monitor?
questions.explanation: Simplex is unidirectional; only one device can transmit, and the other can only receive.

questions.text:
Identify the data flow mode depicted in the following scenario:
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant StationA
    participant StationB
    Note over StationA, StationB: Time 1: A sends to B
    Note over StationA, StationB: Time 2: B sends to A (Not simultaneously)
```
questions.explanation: Half-duplex allows both stations to transmit and receive, but not at the same time.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between transit time and response time in network performance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transit time is for software; Response time is for hardware.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transit time is the travel time for a message; Response time is the elapsed time between inquiry and reply.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transit time measures security; Response time measures reliability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no difference; they are synonymous.

questions.explanation: Transit time is the travel duration for a message, while response time covers the gap between asking a question and receiving the answer.

questions.text: Network reliability is measured by which of the following criteria?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Frequency of failure and recovery time

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of users on the system

questions.explanation: Reliability is measured by the frequency of failure, the time it takes to recover from a failure, and robustness during catastrophe.

questions.text: In a network connection where the entire capacity of the link is reserved for two devices, the connection is:
questions.explanation: A point-to-point connection provides a dedicated link between two devices.

questions.text: Calculate the number of physical links required for a fully connected mesh network of \( n = 10 \) devices operating in duplex mode.
questions.explanation: For a duplex mesh network, the formula is \( \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \). Thus, \( \frac{10(9)}{2} = 45 \).

questions.text: Which topology requires each device to have a dedicated point-to-point link only to a central controller (hub)?
questions.explanation: In a star topology, devices are not linked to each other directly; they connect to a central hub.

questions.text: In a bus topology, what is the term for the connection running between the device and the main cable?
questions.explanation: A drop line is the connection between the device and the main bus cable.

questions.text: A signal in a ring topology travels in how many directions?
questions.explanation: In a ring topology, a signal is passed along the ring in one direction (unidirectional) until it reaches the destination.

questions.text:
A hybrid topology consisting of a star backbone with three bus networks is common. Which of the following best describes this structure?
```mermaid
graph TD
    Hub((Star Hub))
    Bus1[Bus Segment 1]
    Bus2[Bus Segment 2]
    Bus3[Bus Segment 3]
    Hub --- Bus1
    Hub --- Bus2
    Hub --- Bus3
```

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every station is connected to every other station.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A central controller links several bus networks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a simple ring of stars.

questions.explanation: A hybrid topology combines different topologies, such as a star backbone connecting multiple bus networks.

questions.text: Which category of network is typically privately owned and limited to a single office or building?
questions.explanation: LAN (Local Area Network) is privately owned and links devices in a small geographic area like an office or building.

questions.text: Which type of network spans a large geographic area such as a country or continent?
questions.explanation: WAN (Wide Area Network) provides long-distance transmission over large geographic areas.

questions.text: A Metropolitan Area Network (MAN) is most appropriately exemplified by which of the following?

choices.label: D
choices.text: A serial cable between two PCs

questions.explanation: A MAN is a network with a size between a LAN and a WAN, typically covering a town or city, like a cable TV network.

questions.text: Which historical network was the precursor to the Internet and involved only four nodes?
questions.explanation: ARPANET was the first network, starting with four nodes at universities.

questions.text: In the early ARPANET, computers were connected to specialized computers called **____**.
questions.explanation: IMPs were specialized computers used in ARPANET to handle communication between hosts.

questions.text: Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn originally developed TCP, which was later split into two protocols. What was the purpose of the split?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To separate physical layer and data link layer functions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To separate datagram routing (IP) from higher-level functions like error detection (TCP).

choices.label: C
choices.text: To allow encrypted and unencrypted traffic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To support both wireless and wired connections.

questions.explanation: TCP was split into TCP and IP; IP handles datagram routing while TCP handles segmentation, reassembly, and error detection.

questions.text: Which ISP layer provides direct services to end-users?
questions.explanation: Local ISPs provide direct service to end users and connect to regional or national ISPs.

questions.text: The structure or format of the data, such as the rule that the first 8 bits are the source address, refers to which key element of a protocol?
questions.explanation: Syntax refers to the structure or format of the data (the order in which it is presented).

questions.text: Which element of a protocol specifies the meaning of each section of bits and what action should be taken based on that interpretation?
questions.explanation: Semantics refers to the meaning of each section of bits and how the pattern is to be interpreted.

questions.text: Protocol 'Timing' involves two main aspects: when data should be sent and **____**.

choices.label: B
choices.text: How fast it can be sent

choices.label: C
choices.text: How many bits are in the header

questions.explanation: Timing defines when data is sent and the speed at which it is sent (to match sender and receiver).

questions.text: Standards that have been legislated by an officially recognized body are known as:
questions.explanation: De jure standards are those that have been legislated by an officially recognized body.

questions.text: Standards that have been adopted through widespread use but not approved by an organized body are known as:
questions.explanation: De facto standards are those that become standard through popular/widespread use despite no official legislation.

questions.text: Which organization is a government agency that regulates communications technology, such as radio, in the United States?
questions.explanation: The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) is a government regulatory agency in the US.

questions.text: A working document with no official status and a 6-month lifetime in the Internet standards process is called a(n):
questions.explanation: An Internet draft is a work in progress with no official status and a limited 6-month lifetime.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the five components of a data communications system?
questions.explanation: The five components are Message, Sender, Receiver, Transmission Medium, and Protocol. Encryption is a security feature, not a core component.

questions.text: In the context of network criteria, 'Performance' is primarily evaluated by which two metrics?
questions.explanation: Performance is often evaluated by throughput (how much data) and delay (how long it takes).

questions.text: A point-to-point WAN is typically a line leased from a provider that connects a home computer to a(n):
questions.explanation: A point-to-point WAN connects an end-user device or LAN to an Internet Service Provider (ISP).

questions.text: What does 'Accuracy' mean in the context of data communication system effectiveness?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delivering data to the correct user.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delivering data as they are produced.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensuring the message is not intercepted.

questions.explanation: Accuracy means the system must deliver the data accurately; data that has been altered in transmission is unusable.

questions.text: A task divided among multiple computers instead of one single machine is the basis of:
questions.explanation: Distributed computing involves dividing a task among multiple computers connected via a network.

questions.text: Which topology's primary advantage is that it is the most robust, where the failure of one link does not incapacitate the entire system, and every connection is dedicated?
questions.explanation: Mesh topology is highly robust because every device has a dedicated point-to-point link to every other device.

questions.text: In a bus topology, what is the 'tap' used for?

choices.label: B
choices.text: As a connector to the main cable

questions.explanation: In a bus topology, a tap is a connector that splices into the main cable to create a connection for a drop line.

questions.text: Which category of network is generally used to connect regional offices of a telephone company?
questions.explanation: Mesh topology is often used in the connection of telephone regional offices.

questions.text: What happens to a signal in a ring topology when it reaches a device?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is absorbed by the device.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The device acts as a repeater to regenerate and pass the bits.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is broadcast to all other devices in the ring simultaneously.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is sent back to the sender.

questions.explanation: In a ring topology, each device includes a repeater that regenerates the signal and passes it to the next device.

questions.text: A network that connects multiple WANs and LANs is known as an:
questions.explanation: An internetwork, or internet (lowercase 'i'), is formed when two or more networks are connected to communicate with each other.

questions.text: ISO stands for which standard creation committee?
questions.explanation: ISO is the International Organization for Standardization.

questions.text: In the evolution of the Internet, what does RFC stand for?
questions.explanation: RFC stands for Request for Comment, a document published by the Internet authority.

questions.text: Which of the following is a characteristic of a 'Full-Duplex' mode?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only one station can transmit at a time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both stations can transmit and receive simultaneously.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It requires a central hub to manage traffic.

questions.explanation: In full-duplex mode, both stations can transmit and receive signals at the same time, like a telephone network.

questions.text: Performance of a network can be evaluated by transit time and response time. Transit time is affected by:

choices.label: B
choices.text: The type of transmission medium and number of users

questions.explanation: Performance (and thus transit time) depends on factors like the number of users, medium type, hardware capability, and software efficiency.

questions.text: Which topology is easiest to install and reconfigure because adding or removing a device involves only one connection to the hub?
questions.explanation: Star topology is easy to install/reconfigure; only one connection is needed per device to the central hub.

questions.text: A multipoint connection is also referred to as a **____** connection.
questions.explanation: A multipoint connection is also known as a multidrop connection, where more than two devices share a single link.

questions.text: Security involves protecting data from unauthorized access and damage, as well as:
questions.explanation: Network security includes protecting data and implementing policies for recovery from breaches and data losses.

questions.text: Which component of a data communications system is a device that receives the message?
questions.explanation: The receiver is the device (computer, telephone, etc.) that receives the message.

questions.text: Which organization established the Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model in the late 1970s?
questions.explanation: The International Standards Organization (ISO) is the multinational body that developed the OSI model.

questions.text: The OSI model consists of seven distinct layers. Select all layers that belong to this model:
questions.explanation: The seven layers are Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, and Application. 'Internet' is a TCP/IP layer name, and 'User' is not an OSI layer.

questions.text: Which layer is responsible for the movement of individual bits from one hop (node) to the next?
questions.explanation: The Physical layer handles the bit-level transmission across the physical medium.

questions.text: Which of the following are primary responsibilities of the Data Link Layer? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: The Data Link layer is responsible for framing, physical addressing, flow control, error control, and access control over a single link. Routing is a Network layer task, and process-to-process delivery is a Transport layer task.

questions.text: The Network layer is responsible for the delivery of __________ from the source host to the destination host.
questions.explanation: In the OSI model, the Network layer manages the delivery of individual packets from the original source to the final destination.

questions.text: Identify the layer responsible for 'Service-Point Addressing'.
questions.explanation: The Transport layer uses service-point (port) addresses to ensure that a message is delivered to the correct process on a computer.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of the Session Layer?

choices.label: B
choices.text: To provide dialog control and synchronization

questions.explanation: The Session layer manages dialog control (half-duplex/full-duplex) and adds synchronization points (checkpoints) to data streams.

questions.text: Which layer is concerned with the syntax and semantics of the information exchanged?
questions.explanation: The Presentation layer handles translation (syntax), encryption, and compression.

questions.text: Which Application Layer service allows a user to log on to a remote host?
questions.explanation: A Network Virtual Terminal (like Telnet) is the software version of a physical terminal which allows remote logging.

questions.text: When comparing the original TCP/IP protocol suite to the OSI model, which layers of the OSI model are combined into the Application layer of TCP/IP? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: In the TCP/IP suite, the Application layer provides the functions found in the OSI Application, Presentation, and Session layers.

questions.text: The Internetworking Protocol (IP) is described as a __________ and __________ protocol.
questions.explanation: IP is a best-effort, unreliable, and connectionless protocol that transports data in datagrams.

questions.text: Which protocol is used to find the physical address of a node when its internet address is known?
questions.explanation: Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) maps a logical (IP) address to a physical (MAC) address.

questions.text: Which Transport Layer protocol in the TCP/IP suite is connection-oriented and provides reliable delivery?
questions.explanation: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) provides reliable, connection-oriented stream delivery.

questions.text: Identify the protocol that combines the best features of UDP and TCP, specifically supporting newer applications like VoIP.
questions.explanation: Stream Control Transmission Protocol (SCTP) is designed to handle newer application needs by combining features of both TCP and UDP.

questions.text:
Match the address types used in TCP/IP to their typical bit/byte sizes as described in the material:

| Address Type | Format/Size |
| --- | --- |
| Physical Address | 48-bit (6-byte) |
| Port Address | 16-bit |
| Logical Address | (Variable/IP Dependent) |

Based on this, how is a physical address usually represented?

choices.label: B
choices.text: As 12 hexadecimal digits separated by colons

choices.label: C
choices.text: As 4 decimal numbers separated by dots

questions.explanation: A physical (MAC) address is typically written as 12 hexadecimal digits, where every 2 digits (1 byte) are separated by a colon.

questions.text: Which of the following addresses remain the same from the original source to the final destination in a standard internet transmission? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Logical (IP) and Port addresses remain constant from source to destination, whereas physical addresses change from hop to hop (node to node).

questions.text:
Consider the following scenario:

```mermaid
graph LR
    S((Sender)) -- Hop 1 --> R1[Router 1]
    R1 -- Hop 2 --> R2[Router 2]
    R2 -- Hop 3 --> D((Receiver))
```
In this multi-hop environment, what happens to the physical addresses in the frame header at each router?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They remain the same throughout the entire path.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are removed and never replaced.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They change at every hop to represent the current sender and the next receiver.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are converted into port addresses.

questions.explanation: Physical addresses are local to a link. As a packet moves from one link to another via a router, the physical addresses change to reflect the next hop.

questions.text: A URL (e.g., `www.mhhe.com`) or an email address (e.g., `forouzan@fhda.edu`) are examples of which type of address?
questions.explanation: Specific addresses are user-friendly addresses like URLs and email addresses that get converted to port and logical addresses by the computer.

questions.text: Which protocol is specifically mentioned as being used for multicasting (simultaneous transmission to a group)?
questions.explanation: Internet Group Message Protocol (IGMP) facilitates multicasting.

questions.text: If a user application needs to send multimedia data and requires a reduction in the number of bits sent, which OSI layer is responsible for this task?
questions.explanation: Compression, which reduces the number of bits in information, is a responsibility of the Presentation layer.

questions.text: What does the term 'Encapsulation' refer to in the context of the OSI model?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The process of physical bits being converted to light signals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The process where each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the data from the layer above it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The encryption of data at the Presentation layer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The routing of packets through the internet.

questions.explanation: Encapsulation is the process where data from an upper layer is wrapped in the header/trailer of a lower layer protocol.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a function of the Data Link Layer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Error control across a single link

choices.label: C
choices.text: Routing packets to their final destination

questions.explanation: Routing or switching packets to their final destination is the primary function of the Network layer, not the Data Link layer.

questions.text: The Transport layer performs 'Segmentation and Reassembly'. This involves adding __________ to segments to ensure they can be reassembled correctly at the destination.
questions.explanation: Sequence numbers are used by the Transport layer to correctly reassemble segments that may arrive out of order.

questions.text: Which of the following addresses is 16 bits in length and represented by a single decimal number?
questions.explanation: A port address is 16 bits long and is usually represented as a single decimal number (e.g., 80 or 8080).

questions.text: The OSI model is a theoretical model for network communication. Which of the following describes the relationship between ISO and OSI correctly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: OSI is the organization; ISO is the model.

choices.label: B
choices.text: ISO is the organization; OSI is the model.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both are interchangeable names for the same protocol suite.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ISO and OSI are two competing models for the internet.

questions.explanation: ISO (International Standards Organization) is the body that created the OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model.

questions.text: How many layers are typically identified in the TCP/IP protocol suite when it is compared directly to the OSI model in modern literature?
questions.explanation: While the original TCP/IP had four, it is commonly compared as a five-layer model: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, and Application.

questions.text: Which layer is responsible for 'Dialog Control'?
questions.explanation: Dialog control, determining which device transmits when (half-duplex/full-duplex), is a Session layer function.

questions.text: Identify the protocol used for sending query and error reporting messages at the network layer.
questions.explanation: Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP) is used for error reporting and status queries (like ping).

questions.text: In a layered architecture, such as OSI, which of the following is true about peer-to-peer processes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Process at layer \( N \) on one machine communicates directly with the physical medium.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Process at layer \( N \) on one machine communicates with layer \( N \) on another machine via the services of lower layers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: All communication is vertical only within a single machine.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Layer \( N \) always skips the layer immediately below it.

questions.explanation: Peer-to-peer processes are the virtual communication between corresponding layers on different machines, facilitated by the physical movement of data through lower layers.

questions.text: Which of the following are examples of Application Layer directory services?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Distributed database sources for global information

questions.explanation: Directory services provide distributed database sources and access for global information.

questions.text: According to the presentation, what is the primary definition of a transmission medium?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A hardware device used to generate digital signals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anything that can carry information from a source to a destination.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The protocol used to manage communication between nodes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A software layer responsible for error correction.

questions.explanation: Transmission medium is defined as anything that can carry information from a source to a destination, typically located below the physical layer.

questions.text: Select all the types of media classified as 'Guided Media' in the text:
questions.explanation: Guided media provide a physical conduit and include twisted-pair, coaxial, and fiber-optic cables. Radio waves and infrared are unguided (wireless) media.

questions.text: What is the primary reason for twisting the two conductors in a twisted-pair cable?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To increase the physical strength of the cable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To reduce the effect of noise and crosstalk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To allow the cable to carry higher voltages.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To differentiate between the signal wire and the ground reference.

questions.explanation: Twisting the wires ensures that both are equally affected by external noise, which the receiver can then cancel out by taking the difference between the two wires.

questions.text: In a twisted-pair configuration, the receiver uses the _________ between the two wires to determine the signal.
questions.explanation: One wire carries the signal and the other is a ground reference; the receiver calculates the difference to extract the intended signal while minimizing noise.

questions.text: Which of the following are characteristics of Shielded Twisted Pair (STP) compared to Unshielded Twisted Pair (UTP)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has a metal foil or braided-mesh covering.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is less bulky and easier to install.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It reduces interference more effectively than UTP.

questions.explanation: STP includes a metal shield to reduce interference but is more expensive and bulkier than UTP.

questions.text: The 'Gauge' of a wire in a twisted-pair cable refers to its:
questions.explanation: Gauge is a measurement of the thickness of the wire conductor.

questions.text: Which of the following components are found in the construction of a Coaxial Cable?
questions.explanation: Coaxial cables consist of a central core, an insulator, an outer conductor (shield), and an outer casing. Glass cladding is a feature of fiber optics.

questions.text: In coaxial cables, what dual role does the outer conductor serve?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A shield against noise and the second conductor for the circuit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The primary signal carrier and the mechanical protector.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An insulator and a ground reference.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A light reflector and a voltage regulator.

questions.explanation: The metal foil/braid in coax acts as a shield against external EMI and also completes the electrical circuit as the second conductor.

questions.text:
Match the BNC connector type with its specific function:
| Connector Type | Function |
| --- | --- |
| BNC Connector | Connects cable to device (e.g., TV) |
| BNC T Connector | Branches out to a computer/device in Ethernet |
| BNC Terminator | Prevents signal reflection at the cable end |

choices.label: B
choices.text: BNC Terminator is used to connect to the TV.

choices.label: C
choices.text: BNC T Connector is for branching connections.

questions.explanation: The BNC T connector is specifically used for branching in networks, whereas the BNC connector is for endpoints and the terminator is for signal absorption.

questions.text: Compared to twisted-pair cable, coaxial cable generally has:

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lower frequency range and higher cost.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Greater immunity to all types of physical damage.

questions.explanation: Coaxial cable carries higher frequency ranges and offers higher bandwidth, but the signal weakens rapidly (higher attenuation), requiring more repeaters.

questions.text: Fiber-optic cables transmit signals in the form of _________.
questions.explanation: Fiber optics use glass or plastic to transmit information as light signals.

questions.text: Which propagation mode in fiber optics features a core density that decreases gradually from the center to the edge?
questions.explanation: In multimode graded-index fiber, the density is highest at the center and decreases gradually toward the cladding.

questions.text: Why does single-mode fiber have a critical angle close to \( 90^\circ \)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because it uses a very large diameter core.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because of its substantially lower density and smaller diameter.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To allow light to bounce at sharp angles.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To increase the attenuation of the signal.

questions.explanation: Single-mode fiber is manufactured with a much smaller diameter and lower density, making the propagation of beams almost horizontal.

questions.text: Identify the fiber-optic connector that is approximately the same size as an RJ45 connector:
questions.explanation: The MT-RJ is a fiber-optic connector designed to be the same size as the RJ45 used in copper networking.

questions.text: Select the correct advantages of fiber-optic cable from the list below:

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lowest initial cost of all media.

questions.explanation: Fiber is immune to EMI, has high bandwidth, and is corrosive-resistant. However, it is expensive and difficult to install/maintain.

questions.text: What is a significant disadvantage of fiber-optic communication mentioned in the presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High signal attenuation compared to copper.

questions.explanation: Light propagation in fiber is unidirectional; if bidirectional communication is needed, two fibers are required.

questions.text: Unguided media transport electromagnetic waves without a physical conductor. This is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Unguided media refers to wireless communication using air as the medium.

questions.text: Which wireless propagation method involves signals reflecting off the ionosphere to reach greater distances?
questions.explanation: In sky propagation, higher frequency radio waves radiate upward to the ionosphere and are reflected back to Earth.

questions.text: In Line-of-sight (LOS) propagation, what is a limiting factor for the distance between antennas?
questions.explanation: Because signals travel in straight lines, antennas must be tall enough or close enough to overcome the Earth's curvature.

questions.text: Radio waves are defined as electromagnetic waves with frequencies between:
questions.explanation: Radio waves range from 3 kHz to 1 GHz according to the frequency spectrum for wireless communication.

questions.text: What is an advantage of radio waves being omnidirectional?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sending and receiving antennas do not have to be aligned.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are not regulated by authorities like the FCC.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They provide the highest data rates for digital communication.

questions.explanation: Omnidirectional waves propagate in all directions, so antennas do not require precise alignment.

questions.text: Which of the following is a disadvantage of radio waves' ability to penetrate walls?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Signals cannot be received inside buildings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Communication cannot be isolated to just inside a building.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sun's rays will block the signal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The antennas must be facing each other at all times.

questions.explanation: While penetration allows indoor reception, it also means the signal can leak outside the intended area, posing a security or isolation challenge.

questions.text: Microwaves are unidirectional waves with frequencies ranging from _________.
questions.explanation: Microwaves occupy the 1 GHz to 300 GHz portion of the spectrum.

questions.text: Select all typical applications of Microwaves mentioned in the text:
questions.explanation: Microwaves are used for unicast communications like cell phones, satellites, and WLANs. Radio waves are used for multicast like TV and radio.

questions.text: Why can infrared signals NOT be used for communication outside a building?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They cannot travel more than 1 meter.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The sun's rays contain infrared waves that interfere with the signal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They require a vacuum to propagate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Infrared waves are absorbed by oxygen in the open air.

questions.explanation: Infrared is unsuitable for outdoor use because solar radiation includes infrared frequencies which act as a massive source of interference.

questions.text: What is the frequency range for Infrared waves used in communication?
questions.explanation: Infrared waves range from 300 GHz to 400 THz.

questions.text: Which transmission medium class is typically illustrated as being below the physical layer in the OSI model context?
questions.explanation: In network diagrams (like Figure 7.1), the transmission medium is shown as the physical path residing below the physical layer.

questions.text:
Examine the following diagram logic for media classification:
```mermaid
graph TD
    TM[Transmission Media] --> G[Guided]
    TM --> U[Unguided]
    G --> TP[Twisted-Pair]
    G --> CX[Coaxial]
    G --> FO[Fiber-Optic]
    U --> RW[Radio Waves]
    U --> MW[Microwaves]
    U --> IR[Infrared]
```
Which of the following is true based on the categories provided?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Microwaves are a sub-type of Guided media.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coaxial cable is a type of Unguided media.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fiber-optic and Twisted-pair share the same primary classification.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Infrared signals are carried via conductors.

questions.explanation: Both Fiber-optic and Twisted-pair are sub-types of 'Guided' media.

questions.text: In terms of security, which guided medium is noted for having 'greater immunity to tapping'?
questions.explanation: Fiber-optic cables are much harder to tap into without being detected compared to copper cables, which emit electromagnetic signals.

questions.text: What does 'RG' stand for in coaxial cable categorization?
questions.explanation: Coaxial cables are categorized by Radio Government (RG) ratings.

questions.text: What is the primary function of a switch in a network?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To act as a permanent bridge between two fixed devices

choices.label: B
choices.text: To create temporary connections between two or more devices linked to the switch

choices.label: C
choices.text: To provide physical power to the end systems

choices.label: D
choices.text: To act as the final destination for data packets

questions.explanation: A switch is a device capable of creating temporary connections between two or more devices linked to it.

questions.text: According to the taxonomy of switched networks provided in the material, which of the following are the main categories? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: The material classifies switched networks into Circuit-switched and Packet-switched. Packet-switched is further divided into Datagram and Virtual-circuit networks.

questions.text: In a circuit-switched network, how is a link normally divided into multiple channels?
questions.explanation: Each link in a circuit-switched network is normally divided into n channels using Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) or Time Division Multiplexing (TDM).

questions.text: At which layer of the OSI model does circuit switching typically take place?
questions.explanation: Circuit switching takes place at the physical layer.

questions.text: Which of the following are characteristics of a circuit-switched network? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Resources are reserved before starting communication

choices.label: C
choices.text: Each packet can take a different path

choices.label: D
choices.text: Addressing is used only during the setup phase

questions.explanation: In circuit switching, resources are reserved, data is not packetized, and addressing is only used during setup.

questions.text: Why is the efficiency of circuit switching considered low for computer networks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because it requires complex routing tables

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because resources are unavailable to others even if there is no activity for a long time

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because it requires high propagation time

questions.explanation: Resources are allocated for the entire duration of the connection, making them unavailable to other users even during idle periods.

questions.text: What are the components that contribute to the total delay in a circuit-switched network?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Setup Time + Transmission Time + Propagation Time + Teardown Time

questions.explanation: Delay in circuit switching equals the time needed to create the connection, transfer data, and disconnect the circuit.

questions.text: In a datagram network, what criteria is used by the switch for the routing table?
questions.explanation: A switch in a datagram network uses a routing table that is based on the destination address.

questions.text: Which of the following statement is TRUE regarding packets in a datagram network?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They all follow a pre-defined fixed path

choices.label: B
choices.text: Each packet is treated independently from others

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resources are reserved for each packet sequence during a setup phase

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are only used in the physical layer

questions.explanation: Datagram networks are connectionless; each packet is treated independently.

questions.text: At which layer of the OSI model does datagram switching take place?
questions.explanation: Datagram switching is done at the Network layer.

questions.text: Why is a datagram network considered more efficient than a circuit-switched network for data communications?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because there is no propagation delay

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because resources are allocated only when there are packets to be transferred

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because it uses VCIs instead of global addresses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because it requires a setup phase to optimize the path

questions.explanation: Efficiency is high because resources are reallocated when there are no packets to send.

questions.text: What is the primary cause of 'Waiting Time' in a datagram network switch?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The setup of a dedicated channel

choices.label: B
choices.text: The lack of a teardown phase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Packets waiting for resources to be allocated as they arrive (First-Come, First-Served)

choices.label: D
choices.text: The time taken to generate a VCI

questions.explanation: In a datagram network, allocation is done on a first-come, first-served basis, leading to potential waiting times at switches.

questions.text: A virtual-circuit network is described as a cross between which two network types?
questions.explanation: A virtual-circuit network is a cross between a circuit-switched network and a datagram network.

questions.text: Which layer typically implements virtual-circuit switching in Wide Area Networks (WANs)?
questions.explanation: Virtual-circuit switching is implemented in the Data Link layer.

questions.text: What is the difference between Global Addressing and Local Addressing in virtual-circuit networks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Global address is used for data transfer; Local address is used for setup

choices.label: B
choices.text: Global address is unique in the network scope; Local address (VCI) has only switch scope

choices.label: C
