choices.text: Global address changes at every switch; Local address remains constant

questions.explanation: Global address is unique and used only during setup; Local address (VCI) is a small number with scope limited to a single switch.

questions.text:
Consider the following phases in a virtual-circuit network connection. Arrange them in the correct order:
1. Data Transfer Phase
2. Teardown Phase
3. Setup Phase
questions.explanation: The sequence is Setup Phase, then Data Transfer Phase, and finally Teardown Phase.

questions.text: What happens during the Teardown Phase of a virtual-circuit network?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The VCI is assigned to the frames

choices.label: B
choices.text: Switches delete the corresponding entry from their routing/switching tables

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sender sends a global destination address to the receiver

choices.label: D
choices.text: Resources are reserved for the next communication session

questions.explanation: In the teardown phase, switches delete the corresponding entry from their table after a confirmation frame is received.

questions.text: In virtual-circuit switching, although all packets follow the same path, why might they arrive with different delays?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because they are rerouted through different switches

choices.label: B
choices.text: If resource allocation is on demand

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because the VCI changes during the transfer

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because the packet size is variable

questions.explanation: Packets follow the same path but may arrive with different delays if resources are allocated on demand rather than reserved.

questions.text: Which of the following occurs during the 'Setup Request' in virtual-circuit switching? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sender sends a request to the receiver

choices.label: B
choices.text: The request includes global source and destination addresses

choices.label: C
choices.text: Switches create an entry in their switching table

choices.label: D
choices.text: All entries in the switching table are immediately deleted

questions.explanation: The setup request includes global addresses and leads to switches creating table entries.

questions.text: In a virtual-circuit network, when a frame leaves a switch, what happens to its VCI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It remains the same throughout the network

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is replaced with the global destination address

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is changed to a different VCI based on the switch's table

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is removed and never used again

questions.explanation: When a frame arrives at a switch, it has a VCI; when it leaves, it has a different VCI.

questions.text:
Identify the type of network shown in the following workflow:
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as System A
    participant S as Switch
    participant M as System M
    A->>S: Setup Request (includes Address of M)
    S->>M: Setup Request
    M->>S: ACK
    S->>A: ACK (Connection Established)
    A->>M: Data Transfer
    A->>S: Teardown Signal
    S->>M: Release Resources
```
questions.explanation: The presence of a Setup Phase (with ACK) followed by Data Transfer and a dedicated Teardown phase for resource release is characteristic of Circuit Switching.

questions.text: A circuit-switched network connects 8 telephones. If each link uses FDM to connect a maximum of two 4-kHz voice channels, what is the total bandwidth of each link?
questions.explanation: Two 4-kHz channels via FDM equals \( 2 \times 4 \text{ kHz} = 8 \text{ kHz} \).

questions.text: Which of the following switching technologies is the standard for the traditional telephone network?
questions.explanation: Switching at the physical layer in the traditional telephone network uses the circuit-switching approach.

questions.text: In Example 8.2, a company uses two 4x8 switches. What does '4x8' indicate?
questions.explanation: A 4x8 switch means it has 4 inputs and 8 outputs.

questions.text: Why is the delay minimal in a circuit-switched network during the data transfer phase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because there is no waiting time at each node

questions.explanation: In circuit switching, once the connection is established, there is no waiting time at each node/switch.

questions.text: If you are designing a system for the Internet, which switching approach at the network layer is typically used?
questions.explanation: Switching in the Internet is done by using the datagram approach to packet switching at the network layer.

questions.text: Compare Datagram and Virtual-Circuit networks based on the following attributes. Which statements are correct? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Datagram: No setup phase; Virtual-Circuit: Has setup phase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Datagram: Packets follow different paths; Virtual-Circuit: Packets follow the same path

choices.label: C
choices.text: Datagram: Higher delay due to waiting time; Virtual-Circuit: Lower delay after setup

choices.label: D
choices.text: Datagram: Resource reservation; Virtual-Circuit: No resource reservation

questions.explanation: Datagram is connectionless (no setup) and packets can take different paths. Virtual-circuits have a setup phase and packets follow the same path. Datagrams typically have higher delays at switches due to queuing.

questions.text: In a virtual-circuit network, which phase ensures that all switches delete the corresponding entry from their tables?
questions.explanation: During the teardown phase, a request is sent and switches delete the table entry.

questions.text: A T-1 line is used in which example of switching described in the text?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A datagram network for the Internet

choices.label: B
choices.text: A circuit-switched network connecting remote offices

questions.explanation: Example 8.2 describes a circuit-switched network using a T-1 line to connect remote offices.

questions.text: Which type of addressing is used in a virtual-circuit network to actually transfer frames after the path is established?
questions.explanation: Data transfer in virtual-circuit networks uses local addressing via VCIs.

questions.text: What does the term **telecommunication** specifically refer to according to the text?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exchange of binary data between two computers

choices.label: D
choices.text: The use of computers for distributed processing

questions.explanation: As per the introduction slide, 'telecommunication' means communication at a distance.

questions.text: The effectiveness of a data communications system depends on four fundamental characteristics. Select all that apply:
questions.explanation: The text explicitly lists Delivery, Accuracy, Timeliness, and Jitter as the factors for system effectiveness.

questions.text: In the context of data communication effectiveness, how is **Jitter** defined?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of bits lost during transmission

choices.label: B
choices.text: The time it takes for a message to travel between devices

choices.label: C
choices.text: Variation in the packet arrival time

choices.label: D
choices.text: The failure rate of the transmission medium

questions.explanation: Jitter is defined in the text as the variation in packet arrival time.

questions.text: A data communications system is made up of five components. Identify these components from the list below:
questions.explanation: The text lists: 1. Message, 2. Sender, 3. Receiver, 4. Transmission medium, and 5. Protocol as the five components.

questions.text: Which of the following defines the **Protocol** component of a data communications system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The physical path over which data travels

choices.label: B
choices.text: A set of rules that govern data communications

choices.label: C
choices.text: The device that initiates the data transfer

questions.explanation: A protocol is a set of rules that govern data communications.

questions.text: Identify the data flow mode depicted in the following scenario: A walkie-talkie where each person can talk, but only one at a time.
questions.explanation: In half-duplex, each station can both transmit and receive, but not at the same time.

questions.text: Which of these is an example of **Simplex** communication?
questions.explanation: Simplex communication is unidirectional, such as keyboards and monitors.

questions.text: Network reliability is measured by several factors. Select all that apply:

choices.label: B
choices.text: The time it takes a link to recover from a failure

questions.explanation: Reliability is measured by frequency of failure, recovery time, and robustness. Unauthorized access is a security concern, not a reliability metric.

questions.text: Network performance is often evaluated by which two primary metrics?
questions.explanation: The text states performance is evaluated by throughput and delay.

questions.text: What type of connection provides a dedicated link between only two devices?
questions.explanation: Point-to-point is a dedicated link between two devices.

questions.text: In a **Mesh topology** with \( n \) devices, how many physical duplex links are required to connect every device to every other device?
questions.explanation: For duplex connections in a mesh topology, the number of links is calculated as \( n(n-1) / 2 \).

questions.text: Which topology uses a central controller, usually referred to as a **hub**?
questions.explanation: In a star topology, each device has a dedicated point-to-point link only to a central controller (hub).

questions.text: In a **Bus topology**, what are the terms for the lines running between the device and the main cable, and the connector used?
questions.explanation: Nodes are connected to the bus cable by drop lines and taps.

questions.text: Which topology is described as having a signal that passes along the network in one direction from device to device until it reaches its destination?
questions.explanation: In a ring topology, a signal is passed along the ring in one direction until it reaches the destination.

questions.text: A **Hybrid topology** mentioned in the text consists of:

choices.label: A
choices.text: A mesh backbone with star sub-networks

choices.label: B
choices.text: A star backbone with bus networks at the branches

choices.label: C
choices.text: A ring backbone with star sub-networks

choices.label: D
choices.text: A bus backbone with ring networks at the branches

questions.explanation: The text describes a hybrid topology as a main star topology with each branch connecting several stations in a bus topology.

questions.text:
Match the Network Category to its description:

| Category | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1. LAN | i. Covers towns or cities (e.g., Cable TV) |
| 2. WAN | ii. Privately owned, limited to a building or campus |
| 3. MAN | iii. Long transmission over countries or continents |
questions.explanation: LAN (Local Area Network) is for buildings/campuses; WAN (Wide Area Network) is for large geographic areas; MAN (Metropolitan Area Network) covers cities.

questions.text: What are the two types of Wide Area Networks (WANs) described in the slides?
questions.explanation: The slides categorize WANs into Switched WANs (connecting end systems via routers) and Point-to-point WANs (leased lines).

questions.text: What was the name of the first network consisting of four nodes at a group of universities?
questions.explanation: ARPANET was the first network, starting with four nodes at a group of universities.

questions.text: In early ARPANET, each computer was connected to a specialized computer called an **IMP**. What does IMP stand for?
questions.explanation: The text states: 'Each computer is connected to a specialized computer, called interface message processor (IMP).'

questions.text: The original Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) was later split into two protocols. What are they and what are their primary responsibilities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: IP for error detection; TCP for routing

choices.label: B
choices.text: TCP for datagram routing; IP for segmentation

choices.label: C
choices.text: IP for datagram routing; TCP for segmentation, reassembly, and error detection

choices.label: D
choices.text: UDP for speed; TCP for reliability

questions.explanation: TCP was split into TCP and IP. IP handles routing while TCP handles higher-level functions like segmentation and error detection.

questions.text: Arrange the hierarchy of Internet Service Providers (ISPs) from top-level to the one serving the end user:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Regional ISP -> National ISP -> Local ISP

choices.label: B
choices.text: National ISP -> Regional ISP -> Local ISP

choices.label: C
choices.text: Local ISP -> Regional ISP -> National ISP

choices.label: D
choices.text: National ISP -> Local ISP -> Regional ISP

questions.explanation: The hierarchy according to the text is National ISPs, which connect to Regional ISPs, which then connect to Local ISPs that serve end users.

questions.text: Protocols have three key elements. Which element refers to the **meaning** of each section of bits?
questions.explanation: Semantics refers to the meaning of each section of bits and what action is to be taken.

questions.text: What element of a protocol deals with the structure or format of the data (e.g., the first 8 bits are the source address)?
questions.explanation: Syntax refers to the structure or format of the data.

questions.text:
Match the standards category to its definition:

1. **De facto**
2. **De jure**

i. Standards legislated by an officially recognized body.
ii. Standards adopted through widespread use but not officially approved.
questions.explanation: De facto standards are by use; De jure standards are by law/official body.

questions.text: What is an **Internet Draft** according to the standards maturity process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A finished standard ready for implementation

choices.label: B
choices.text: A working document with no official status and a 6-month lifetime

choices.label: C
choices.text: A request for comments from the general public

choices.label: D
choices.text: A legally binding contract for ISPs

questions.explanation: An Internet Draft is a working document with no official status and a 6-month lifetime.

questions.text: Which organization is a regulatory agency in the US that regulates radio and communications technology?
questions.explanation: The text mentions the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) as a regulatory agency in the US.

questions.text: Which element of a protocol addresses when data should be sent and how fast it can be sent?
questions.explanation: Timing refers to when data should be sent and how fast it can be sent (matching speed).

questions.text: According to the slides, what is the typical speed of a Local Area Network (LAN)?
questions.explanation: The text states LAN speed is normally 100 or 1000 Mbps.

questions.text: A task is divided among multiple computers in a network. This concept is known as:
questions.explanation: Distributed computing is when a task is divided among multiple computers.

questions.text: What is the primary function of a **Router** in a switched WAN?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To connect end systems to the network

choices.label: B
choices.text: To convert analog signals to digital

choices.label: C
choices.text: To create the physical cable path

choices.label: D
choices.text: To act as a central hub in a star topology

questions.explanation: In a switched WAN, the router is part of the system that connects the end systems.
